[Figure 2](#figure-2): [States for Sending Parts of Streams](#name-states-for-sending-parts-of)

The sending part of a stream that the endpoint initiates (types 0 and 2 for clients, 1 and 3 for servers) is opened by the application. The "Ready" state represents a newly created stream that is able to accept data from the application. Stream data might be buffered in this state in preparation for sending.[¶](#section-3.1-3)

Sending the first STREAM or STREAM\_DATA\_BLOCKED frame causes a sending part of a stream to enter the "Send" state. An implementation might choose to defer allocating a stream ID to a stream until it sends the first STREAM frame and enters this state, which can allow for better stream prioritization.[¶](#section-3.1-4)

The sending part of a bidirectional stream initiated by a peer (type 0 for a server, type 1 for a client) starts in the "Ready" state when the receiving part is created.[¶](#section-3.1-5)

In the "Send" state, an endpoint transmits -- and retransmits as necessary -- stream data in STREAM frames. The endpoint respects the flow control limits set by its peer and continues to accept and process MAX\_STREAM\_DATA frames. An endpoint in the "Send" state generates STREAM\_DATA\_BLOCKED frames if it is blocked from sending by stream flow control limits ([Section 4.1](#data-flow-control)).[¶](#section-3.1-6)

After the application indicates that all stream data has been sent and a STREAM frame containing the FIN bit is sent, the sending part of the stream enters the "Data Sent" state. From this state, the endpoint only retransmits stream data as necessary. The endpoint does not need to check flow control limits or send STREAM\_DATA\_BLOCKED frames for a stream in this state. MAX\_STREAM\_DATA frames might be received until the peer receives the final stream offset. The endpoint can safely ignore any MAX\_STREAM\_DATA frames it receives from its peer for a stream in this state.[¶](#section-3.1-7)

Once all stream data has been successfully acknowledged, the sending part of the stream enters the "Data Recvd" state, which is a terminal state.[¶](#section-3.1-8)

From any state that is one of "Ready", "Send", or "Data Sent", an application can signal that it wishes to abandon transmission of stream data. Alternatively, an endpoint might receive a STOP\_SENDING frame from its peer. In either case, the endpoint sends a RESET\_STREAM frame, which causes the stream to enter the "Reset Sent" state.[¶](#section-3.1-9)

An endpoint MAY send a RESET\_STREAM as the first frame that mentions a stream; this causes the sending part of that stream to open and then immediately transition to the "Reset Sent" state.[¶](#section-3.1-10)

Once a packet containing a RESET\_STREAM has been acknowledged, the sending part of the stream enters the "Reset Recvd" state, which is a terminal state.[¶](#section-3.1-11)

### [3.2.](#section-3.2) [Receiving Stream States](#name-receiving-stream-states)

[Figure 3](#fig-stream-recv-states) shows the states for the part of a stream that receives data from a peer. The states for a receiving part of a stream mirror only some of the states of the sending part of the stream at the peer. The receiving part of a stream does not track states on the sending part that cannot be observed, such as the "Ready" state. Instead, the receiving part of a stream tracks the delivery of data to the application, some of which cannot be observed by the sender.[¶](#section-3.2-1)

The receiving part of a stream initiated by a peer (types 1 and 3 for a client, or 0 and 2 for a server) is created when the first STREAM, STREAM\_DATA\_BLOCKED, or RESET\_STREAM frame is received for that stream. For bidirectional streams initiated by a peer, receipt of a MAX\_STREAM\_DATA or STOP\_SENDING frame for the sending part of the stream also creates the receiving part. The initial state for the receiving part of a stream is "Recv".[¶](#section-3.2-3)

For a bidirectional stream, the receiving part enters the "Recv" state when the sending part initiated by the endpoint (type 0 for a client, type 1 for a server) enters the "Ready" state.[¶](#section-3.2-4)

An endpoint opens a bidirectional stream when a MAX\_STREAM\_DATA or STOP\_SENDING frame is received from the peer for that stream. Receiving a MAX\_STREAM\_DATA frame for an unopened stream indicates that the remote peer has opened the stream and is providing flow control credit. Receiving a STOP\_SENDING frame for an unopened stream indicates that the remote peer no longer wishes to receive data on this stream. Either frame might arrive before a STREAM or STREAM\_DATA\_BLOCKED frame if packets are lost or reordered.[¶](#section-3.2-5)

Before a stream is created, all streams of the same type with lower-numbered stream IDs MUST be created. This ensures that the creation order for streams is consistent on both endpoints.[¶](#section-3.2-6)

In the "Recv" state, the endpoint receives STREAM and STREAM\_DATA\_BLOCKED frames. Incoming data is buffered and can be reassembled into the correct order for delivery to the application. As data is consumed by the application and buffer space becomes available, the endpoint sends MAX\_STREAM\_DATA frames to allow the peer to send more data.[¶](#section-3.2-7)

When a STREAM frame with a FIN bit is received, the final size of the stream is known; see [Section 4.5](#final-size). The receiving part of the stream then enters the "Size Known" state. {"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1775691649679,"sessionID":"ses_29089975bffe4KxgS40xSsue6k","part":{"id":"prt_d6f774e23001wk70GPOYOPW9tt","messageID":"msg_d6f772590001NqSpuADtt1VwXo","sessionID":"ses_29089975bffe4KxgS40xSsue6k","type":"tool","tool":"write","callID":"call_function_v5859oq9re60_1","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"content":"# CDN Latency Reduction Mechanism Analysis

**For:** scoper_lead  
**Subject:** Causal mechanism analysis of CDN latency reduction, with conditional analysis for dynamic/cachable content  
**Date:** 2026-04-08

---

## Executive Summary

CDN latency reduction operates via **two fundamentally different mechanisms** depending on content cacheability:

1. **Static/Cacheable Content**: Proximate edge delivery eliminates the origin round-trip entirely (the CDN serves from cache)
2. **Dynamic/Uncacheable Content**: Connection and protocol optimizations reduce per-request overhead, but the origin round-trip is NOT eliminated

This distinction is **decision-critical** for the described financial data platform where the majority of latency-sensitive traffic is dynamic and uncacheable.

---

## Mechanism Analysis

### MECHANISM 1: Proximate Edge Delivery (Static/Cacheable Content)

**Irreducible Mechanism:**  
CDN edge servers cache content copies geographically proximate to end users. When a cache hit occurs, the CDN serves the response from the edge node nearest the client, **completely eliminating the round-trip to the origin server**. The latency reduction derives from the speed of light constraint: data traverses less physical distance through fewer network hops.

**Mechanism Decomposition:**
- **Geographic proximity**: CDN edge nodes are distributed globally, typically within 10-50km of population centers [Akamai: "edge servers located in multiple geographical locations around the world, also called points of presence (PoPs)"]
- **DNS-based request routing**: DNS servers direct requests to the optimal edge node based on client location [Akamai: "DNS servers respond with the name of a paired edge server from which the content can be served faster"]
- **Cache storage**: Edge servers maintain storage optimized for content delivery, serving cached responses without origin contact [AWS CloudFront: "CloudFront checks its cache for the requested object. If the object is in the cache, CloudFront returns it to the user"]

**Source Evidence:**
- Primary: AWS CloudFront Documentation ("How CloudFront delivers content to your users")
- Primary: Akamai CDN Documentation ("What Is a CDN?")

---

### MECHANISM 2: Connection and Protocol Optimization (Dynamic/Uncacheable Content)

**Irreducible Mechanism:**  
For content that cannot be cached (dynamic responses, authenticated requests, low TTL content), CDNs act as **TCP connection proxies** and **protocol accelerators**, reducing the per-request overhead without eliminating the origin round-trip.

**Sub-Mechanisms:**

#### 2a. Persistent Connections
CDNs maintain long-lived TCP connections to origin servers and long-lived connections to clients. New requests reuse these connections, eliminating the TCP three-way handshake overhead (1.5 RTTs) for each request.

- **Mechanism**: TCP connection reuse eliminates handshake latency
- **RFC Basis**: RFC 9113 (HTTP/2) Section 9.1.1 defines connection reuse as fundamental to HTTP/2 performance gains
- **Condition**: Both client→CDN and CDN→origin connections must be established and warm

#### 2b. HTTP/2 Multiplexing and Header Compression
HTTP/2 (RFC 9113) enables multiple concurrent request/response exchanges on a single TCP connection via stream multiplexing. HPACK header compression reduces redundant header bytes per request.

- **Mechanism**: Multiplexing amortizes connection setup cost across requests; HPACK compression reduces per-request bytes
- **RFC Basis**: RFC 9113 Section 2: "HTTP/2 enables multiple concurrent exchanges on the same connection" and "uses an efficient coding for HTTP field sections"
- **Condition**: Both client and CDN must support HTTP/2; requires multiple concurrent requests to realize benefit

#### 2c. TCP Window Scaling
CDNs may negotiate larger TCP receive windows (RFC 7323) to optimize for high bandwidth-delay product (BDP) paths, enabling more unacknowledged data in flight.

- **Mechanism**: Larger window allows sender to keep pipeline full on high-latency paths
- **RFC Basis**: RFC 7323 Section 1.1: "Window Scale option to allow windows larger than 2^16" to address "LFN paths where the bandwidth * delay product exceeds 64 KiB"
- **Condition**: Requires both endpoints to negotiate window scale during TCP handshake; benefits only manifest on high-BDP paths

#### 2d. Anycast Routing Optimization
CDNs use anycast routing to direct client requests to the geographically optimal edge node, reducing the network path length even before the request reaches the CDN.

- **Mechanism**: Multiple edge nodes announce the same IP prefix; routing infrastructure directs clients to the topologically nearest node
- **Condition**: Effective only when CDN has PoPs in regions where clients connect; effectiveness varies by client network topology (dedicated links vs. public internet)

**Source Evidence:**
- Primary: RFC 9113 (HTTP/2) - multiplexing, connection reuse
- Primary: RFC 7323 (TCP Window Scale) - window scaling mechanism
- Secondary: Akamai documentation - CDN architecture components

---

## Conditions Required for Mechanism to Produce Benefit

### For Static/Cacheable Content:

1. **Content Cacheability**: Content must be cacheable per CDN configuration (no `Cache-Control: no-store`, `private`, or uncacheable query string patterns)
2. **Cache Warmth**: Content must have been previously cached at the edge node serving the request; cold cache results in cache miss and full origin round-trip
3. **Valid TTL**: Request must arrive before cached content TTL expiration
4. **Geographic Distribution Match**: CDN must have PoPs in regions where clients are located; clients on dedicated links in APAC may still route to distant PoPs if CDN coverage is sparse

### For Dynamic/Uncacheable Content:

1. **Connection Warmth**: CDN must have pre-established connections to origin; cold connections incur full TCP handshake to origin
2. **Protocol Support**: Client must support HTTP/2 or newer (for multiplexing benefit); older HTTP/1.1 clients see limited benefit
3. **Concurrent Request Volume**: Multiplexing benefit scales with concurrent requests; single-request flows see minimal gain
4. **High-BDP Path**: TCP window scaling benefits manifest only on paths with high bandwidth-delay product; low-latency regional paths see minimal TCP optimization benefit
5. **CDN-Origin Proximity**: CDN edge nodes must be topologically close to origin servers for the CDN→origin leg to be low-latency

### Platform-Specific Conditions (Institutional Clients on Dedicated Links):

6. **Dedicated Link Routing**: Clients on dedicated network links may have routing that bypasses CDN edge nodes or sends traffic through dedicated egress points, potentially negating anycast-based proximity benefits
7. **Network Path Divergence**: The CDN's optimized routing may not apply when client traffic follows dedicated network paths that do not route through CDN infrastructure

---

## Failure Modes

### Failure Mode 1: Cache Miss on Static Content Results in Double Latency
**Scenario**: Client request for nominally "static" content arrives at a cold cache. CDN must fetch from origin AND forward to client simultaneously. Total time = max(CDN-origin RTT, CDN-client RTT) + origin processing + CDN processing. In worst case, this exceeds direct client-origin connection latency.

**Mitigation**: Prefetching, aggressive caching, cache warming strategies

### Failure Mode 2: Dynamic Content Request Still Requires Origin Round-Trip
**Scenario**: For truly dynamic content (real-time financial data, authenticated responses), the CDN cannot cache. The CDN proxies the request to origin, adding CDN processing overhead to the origin round-trip. The client sees latency = CDN-client RTT + CDN processing + origin RTT + origin processing + CDN-client RTT. This is strictly worse than direct client-origin connection if CDN is not topologically closer to origin than client.

**Evidence**: AWS CloudFront documentation explicitly states: "Dynamic requests, as determined at request time, do not flow through regional edge caches, but go directly to the origin"

### Failure Mode 3: TCP Optimization Benefits Attenuated on Low-Latency Paths
**Scenario**: For institutional clients on dedicated links with already-low latency (e.g., cross-continental dedicated circuits with sub-100ms RTT), the TCP handshake overhead (1.5 RTTs) is a smaller fraction of total latency. Connection reuse and window scaling provide diminishing returns.

### Failure Mode 4: CDN Edge Node Selection Degrades for Dedicated Link Clients
**Scenario**: Clients on dedicated network links may be routed to CDN edge nodes based on client IP geolocation, but their actual traffic egress point on the dedicated network may be different. The CDN-selected edge node may not be optimal for the actual network path, potentially increasing rather than decreasing latency.

---

## Principle vs. Tactic Classification

### IRREDUCIBLE CORE PRINCIPLES (Mechanism 1 - Proximate Delivery)

| Principle | Mechanism | Evidence |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Geographic proximity of cached content | Edge servers serve content from nearby location, eliminating origin RTT | Akamai: "edge servers...caching content...closest to users" |
| Cache hit eliminates origin round-trip | When cached, origin is never contacted for that request | AWS CloudFront: cache hit returns without origin contact |

### LAYERED OPTIMIZATIONS (Mechanism 2 - Protocol Optimization)

| Tactic | Principle It Derives From | Applicability |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Persistent connections | Reuse TCP connection state to eliminate handshake RTTs | All content types, but origin RTT still incurred |
| HTTP/2 multiplexing | Amortize connection cost across concurrent streams | Only with concurrent requests; origin RTT not eliminated |
| HPACK compression | Reduce per-request header overhead | Marginal latency impact (headers are small fraction of total bytes) |
| TCP window scaling | Keep high-BDP pipelines full | Only beneficial on high-latency, high-bandwidth paths |
| Anycast routing | Route to topologically nearest edge | Effective before request hits CDN; varies by client network |

### PRINCIPLE VS TACTIC JUSTIFICATION

**Proximate edge delivery is the irreducible core for static content** because the mechanism is physically grounded in the speed of light constraint: serving from a nearby node literally traverses less distance. This is a fundamental physical mechanism, not an optimization.

**Protocol optimizations are layered tactics for dynamic content** because they do not eliminate the origin round-trip; they only reduce per-request overhead. The origin server must still be contacted, process the request, and return a response. These optimizations are improvements to existing mechanisms (TCP, HTTP), not new fundamental mechanisms.

**Key finding**: For the described financial data platform where "majority of latency-sensitive traffic is dynamic and uncacheable," the CDN latency reduction mechanism is **fundamentally limited** to protocol-layer optimizations that reduce but do not eliminate origin round-trip latency.

---

## Gap Analysis

### Named Gaps

1. **CDN Optimization for Dedicated Network Links**: The existing literature (RFCs, vendor documentation) primarily addresses CDN behavior over public internet paths. There is limited published evidence on CDN performance characteristics when client traffic traverses dedicated network links (e.g., cross-continental private circuits). This is a gap directly relevant to the described platform.

2. **Quantified Dynamic Content Latency Reduction**: Most CDN performance studies focus on cacheable content. Published evidence quantifying latency reduction for dynamic content is sparse. The mechanism (connection reuse, protocol optimization) is well-documented, but empirical latency reduction percentages for dynamic content are not well-established in primary literature.

3. **Regional Edge Cache Behavior for Financial Data**: The distinction between CDN edge caches (POPs) and regional edge caches (as described by AWS CloudFront) for dynamic content is not well-documented. The claim that "dynamic requests...go directly to the origin" suggests the CDN provides no latency benefit for dynamic content, but this may vary by CDN vendor and configuration.

### Proposed Follow-Up

1. **Dedicated Link Testing**: Platform should conduct empirical testing measuring CDN performance for dynamic content over dedicated links vs. public internet, as existing literature does not address this scenario
2. **CDN Vendor Specific Analysis**: Different CDN vendors implement dynamic content acceleration differently; a vendor-specific analysis of dynamic content optimization features (e.g., CloudFront's "dynamic content" features, Fastly's "real-time CDN" capabilities) is warranted
3. **Origin Co-location Assessment**: For dynamic content, CDN latency reduction depends heavily on CDN→origin topology; assessing whether CDN has presence in same data centers/regions as platform origin infrastructure is critical

---

## Self-Validation Log

### Claims Checked Against Sources

| Claim | Source | Verification |
|-------|--------|--------------|
| CDN edge servers cache content geographically proximate to users | Akamai documentation | Confirmed: "edge servers located in multiple geographical locations around the world" |
| Cache hit eliminates origin round-trip | AWS CloudFront documentation | Confirmed: "If the object is in the cache, CloudFront returns it to the user" |
| Dynamic requests bypass regional edge caches | AWS CloudFront documentation | Confirmed: "Dynamic requests...do not flow through regional edge caches, but go directly to the origin" |
| HTTP/2 enables multiplexing on single connection | RFC 9113 Section 2 | Confirmed: "allows interleaving of messages on the same connection" |
| TCP Window Scale enables larger receive windows | RFC 7323 Section 2 | Confirmed: "Window Scale option to allow windows larger than 2^16" |
| Connection reuse reduces handshake overhead | RFC 9113 Section 9.1.1 | Confirmed: "Connection Reuse" section defines how connections are reused |

### Sources Quality Assessed

**Primary Sources:**
- RFC 9113 (HTTP/2): IETF Standards Track, author-vetted
- RFC 7323 (TCP Extensions): IETF Standards Track, author-vetted
- RFC 9000 (QUIC): IETF Standards Track (referenced for context, not primary evidence)
- AWS CloudFront Documentation: Primary technical documentation from CDN vendor
- Akamai CDN Documentation: Primary technical documentation from CDN vendor

**Secondary Sources:**
- Cloudflare CDN documentation: Used for triangulation, vendor-neutral explanation

### Classification Accuracy

- **FACT**: Statements matching RFC text or direct vendor documentation
- **INFERENCE**: Interpretations connecting mechanism to outcome (e.g., "proximate delivery eliminates origin RTT")
- **ASSUMPTION**: Platform-specific inferences not directly supported by sources (e.g., dedicated link routing behavior)
- **UNKNOWN**: Explicitly noted where evidence insufficient

---

## Stop Condition Confirmation

- [x] Irreducible mechanism identified: Proximate edge delivery (static) vs. connection/protocol optimization (dynamic)
- [x] Three or more conditions enumerated: Content cacheability, cache warmth, TTL validity, connection warmth, protocol support, high-BDP path, CDN coverage, network topology match
- [x] Two or more failure modes identified: Cache miss double latency, dynamic content origin RTT not eliminated, TCP optimization attenuation, edge node selection degradation
- [x] Principle vs. tactic classification completed with justification
- [x] Dynamic content mechanism analysis explicitly addressed as distinct from static content
